name: Skinwalker
description: Skinwalkers are malevolent shape-shifting witches found in Navajo and other Native American traditions. They are said to be individuals who have attained a high level of spiritual power, but have chosen to use it for evil purposes. They can transform into animals, most commonly wolves, coyotes, foxes, bears, or birds, in order to stalk, harass, and harm people. Skinwalkers are often associated with witchcraft and dark magic, and are believed to be able to inflict illness, bad luck, and even death upon their victims. They are feared and reviled within their communities, and are often seen as a symbol of betrayal and corruption. The process of becoming a skinwalker is shrouded in secrecy, involving forbidden rituals and the violation of sacred taboos. Their motivations are typically rooted in jealousy, revenge, or a desire for power. They are said to be incredibly difficult to kill, possessing supernatural resilience and cunning. Traditional Navajo beliefs dictate that even mentioning the name of a skinwalker can attract their attention, leading to misfortune. This is why many Navajo people are reluctant to discuss them openly with outsiders. They are not simply animals wearing skins, but humans who have fully transformed, retaining their intelligence and malice.
subtitle: Shape-shifting witch of Navajo lore
appearance: While in human form, a skinwalker may appear as an ordinary person, although some claim they possess an unsettling gaze or an unnatural aura. In animal form, they often appear larger and more menacing than a typical animal of that species. They may also have glowing red eyes, or exhibit unnatural behaviors. Sometimes, they are described as wearing the skin of the animal they have transformed into, though it may be ill-fitting or inside out.
habitat: Skinwalkers are most commonly associated with the Navajo Nation, which spans parts of Arizona, New Mexico, and Utah. They are believed to inhabit remote and isolated areas, such as forests, canyons, and deserts. They are said to prefer places where they can observe their victims unnoticed, and where they can easily disappear into the landscape.
behavior: Skinwalkers are primarily motivated by malice and a desire to cause harm. They stalk and harass their victims, often targeting individuals or families they have a grudge against. They may attempt to frighten, injure, or even kill their victims, using their shape-shifting abilities to their advantage. They are also known to steal livestock, vandalize property, and spread disease. They are cunning and manipulative, and often attempt to deceive their victims by mimicking the voices of loved ones or animals.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of the Skinwalker
keyMyths.description: The origin of skinwalkers is often linked to individuals who have broken sacred taboos or engaged in forbidden rituals, betraying their community and embracing dark magic. This act grants them the power to shape-shift but at the cost of their humanity and spiritual well-being.

keyMyths.title: Skinwalker Encounters
keyMyths.description: Stories of skinwalker encounters often involve strange noises, unexplained animal deaths, and sightings of unusual creatures behaving in unnatural ways. These encounters instill fear and unease, highlighting the power and malevolence associated with these beings.

sources.author: Trudy Griffin-Pierce
sources.description: Provides cultural context and information on Navajo beliefs.
sources.text: Provides cultural context and information on Navajo beliefs.
